meta_title: For Students: analytics setup to Solve no marketing skills (Beginner Case Study)
meta_description: Learn how to analytics setup and overcome no marketing skills. A Beginner-level Case Study using Antigravity and Claude Code.

content.seo_title: For Students: Analytics Setup to Solve No Marketing Skills
content.seo_description: Stop guessing your app's growth. Learn how to implement data-driven analytics to overcome a lack of marketing skills using Antigravity and Claude Code.
content.answer_block: You don't need a marketing degree to grow; you need data. By implementing event-based analytics, you transform user behavior into actionable product insights. This guide shows you how to automate your tracking setup using Claude Code, allowing you to iterate based on what users actually do, not what you guess.
content.intro:
You’ve spent weeks crafting the perfect architecture, writing clean code, and finally pushing your MVP to production. Then, the silence hits. You have zero traffic and no marketing budget. Most students freeze here, believing they need a massive social media following or a paid ads budget to succeed. But the secret isn't more noise; it's better listening.

Great engineers don't market; they optimize through data. By setting up a robust analytics pipeline, you turn your app into a feedback loop. You’ll stop wondering why users churn and start seeing exactly where they get stuck. This isn't just about tracking clicks—it's about building a product that speaks for itself through evidence-based iteration.

steps.title: Define Your North Star Metric
steps.description: Before writing code, identify the one action that proves user value. For a student project, this is usually 'Account Created' or 'First Task Completed'.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Analyze my current codebase and suggest 3 high-impact user events to track based on my project type. Keep it minimal to avoid data bloat.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run --plan 'Identify event tracking opportunities in src/components'

steps.title: Configure Analytics Provider
steps.description: Integrate a lightweight provider like PostHog or Mixpanel. We focus on low-latency SDKs that don't bloat your bundle size.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Generate a modular analytics service wrapper in TypeScript that handles event initialization and user identity tracking.

steps.title: Instrument Critical User Flows
steps.description: Use decorators or a custom hook to wrap your core UI interactions with event triggers without cluttering your business logic.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Create a custom React hook 'useTrackEvent' that simplifies sending events to the service wrapper created in step 2.
steps.claude_code_command: claude edit 'Implement useTrackEvent hook in src/hooks and apply to Login and Signup buttons'

steps.title: Implement Session Replay
steps.description: Visual context is worth more than a thousand logs. Set up session recording to see how users interact with your UI.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Provide the configuration script to enable session recording for my specific framework, ensuring PII masking is active.
steps.claude_code_command: claude run 'Configure session recording with PII masking in app initialization'

steps.title: Verify Data Pipeline
steps.description: Automate a test sequence to trigger every tracked event and verify they reach the dashboard correctly.
steps.antigravity_prompt: Write a Playwright test script that simulates a user journey and asserts that the analytics events are fired.

pro_tips: Always mask PII (Personally Identifiable Information) at the source; never send emails or names to your analytics dashboard.
Track 'failed' events as aggressively as 'success' events; failures tell you exactly where your UX is broken.
Use unique session IDs to correlate anonymous traffic before a user signs up to track the full conversion funnel.
Keep your event schema in a single config file; changing variable names later is a nightmare for data integrity.
Review your dashboard weekly for 15 minutes; if a metric doesn't influence your next code commit, stop tracking it.
content.next_steps: Once your data starts flowing, identify the 20% of your UI that causes 80% of your drop-offs and prioritize fixing those flows in your next sprint.
content.conversion_block: You now have the technical foundation to build a growth-oriented product that speaks for itself. Don't let the lack of a marketing team stop your momentum—let your data do the heavy lifting. Ready to build? Join our AI Builder community → Start your first project today.
